---
title: "Multivariate Classification and Differential Expression Analysis of Whole Blood RNA-seq Data in Type 1 Diabetes"
author: "Gloria Jackson"
Instructor: "Anthony Fodor, PhD"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: pdf_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

```{r load libraries, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, include=FALSE}
# load libraries here
library(BiocManager)
library(DESeq2)
library(edgeR)
library(vsn)
library(randomForest)
library(caret)
library(ggplot2)
```


## Data Preparation

```{r data prep}
# load dataset
count_matrix <- read.table("GSE123658_raw_counts_GRCh38.p13_NCBI.tsv", header = TRUE, row.names = 1, sep = "\t")

# checkpoint--should show 39376 rows and 82 columns
#dim(count_matrix)

# create the condition vector
# ensure the order matches the columns in count_matrix
condition_vector <- factor(c(rep("Healthy", 43), rep("T1D", 39)))

# create the metadata object
metadata <- data.frame(
  row.names = colnames(count_matrix), 
  condition = condition_vector
)

# filter low counts
# rule: keep genes with at least 10 counts in at least 39 samples
keep <- rowSums(count_matrix >= 10) >= 39
counts_filtered <- count_matrix[keep, ]

# checkpoint--see how many genes are left (it should be much less than 39,376)
nrow(counts_filtered)

# normalize + stabilize
# create the DESeq object
dds <- DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(countData = counts_filtered,
                              colData = metadata,
                              design = ~ condition)

# run the variance stabilizing transformation
# this handles normalization + log-transformation in one go-lets goooo
vsd <- vst(dds, blind = FALSE)

# extract the clean data for PCA & random forest
clean_data <- assay(vsd)
```

# PCA Code

```{python code for PCA using scikit-learn (figure 1 in paper)}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler # Added for proper scaling
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# 1. Load the data
data = pd.read_csv('/Users/gloriajackson/Desktop/26_spring/binf_6310-stats/vst_normalized_counts.csv', index_col=0)
metadata = pd.read_csv('/Users/gloriajackson/Desktop/26_spring/binf_6310-stats/sample_metadata.csv', index_col=0)

# 2. Use ALL genes (No subsetting)
# Transpose so samples are rows (82) and genes are columns (18,015)
X = data.T 

# 3. Standardize the data 
# This ensures each gene has a mean of 0 and variance of 1.
# This is highly recommended when using the full genome to prevent 
# highly expressed genes from drowning out the signal.
X_scaled = StandardScaler().fit_transform(X)

# 4. Run PCA
pca = PCA(n_components=2)
components = pca.fit_transform(X_scaled)

# 5. Create a plotting dataframe
pca_df = pd.DataFrame(data=components, columns=['PC1', 'PC2'], index=X.index)

# Ensure metadata alignment
pca_df['condition'] = metadata.loc[pca_df.index, 'condition']

# 6. Calculate variance explained
var_exp = pca.explained_variance_ratio_ * 100

# 7. Plot
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
sns.scatterplot(data=pca_df, x='PC1', y='PC2', hue='condition', palette={'T1D': 'blue', 'Healthy': 'red'}, s=100)
plt.title(f'PCA using ALL Genes ({X.shape[1]} features)\nPC1: {var_exp[0]:.1f}%, PC2: {var_exp[1]:.1f}%')
plt.xlabel(f'PC1 ({var_exp[0]:.1f}%)')
plt.ylabel(f'PC2 ({var_exp[1]:.1f}%)')
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.show()
```


```{python code for silhouette method (figure 3 in paper)}
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.metrics import silhouette_score

# 1. Determine optimal K using Silhouette Method
sil_scores = []
k_range = range(2, 7) # Checking 2 to 6 clusters

for k in k_range:
    kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=k, init='k-means++', n_init=10, random_state=42)
    labels = kmeans.fit_predict(X_scaled)
    score = silhouette_score(X_scaled, labels)
    sil_scores.append(score)
    print(f"For k={k}, Silhouette Score: {score:.4f}")

# Plot the Silhouette scores to see the 'peak'
plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
plt.plot(k_range, sil_scores, 'bo-')
plt.xlabel('Number of Clusters (k)')
plt.ylabel('Silhouette Score')
plt.title('Silhouette Method for Optimal K')
plt.show()

# 2. Run K-Means with k=3 (as found in the paper)
kmeans_final = KMeans(n_clusters=2, init='k-means++', n_init=10, random_state=42)
pca_df['cluster'] = kmeans_final.fit_predict(X_scaled)

```


```{python code for k=2 cluster plot (figure 5 in paper)}
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans

# 1. Force k=3 to match the paper
k = 2
kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=k, init='k-means++', n_init=10, random_state=42)
pca_df['cluster'] = kmeans.fit_predict(X_scaled)

# 2. Calculate the "Majority Percentages" for the legend
cluster_labels = {}
for i in range(k):
    # Get samples in this cluster
    cluster_samples = pca_df[pca_df['cluster'] == i]
    total = len(cluster_samples)
    
    # Count T1D vs Healthy
    counts = cluster_samples['condition'].value_counts()
    majority_label = counts.idxmax() # e.g., 'T1D'
    percentage = (counts.max() / total) * 100
    
    # Create the legend string
    cluster_labels[i] = f"Cluster {i}: {percentage:.1f}% {majority_label}"

# 3. Map the new labels back to a column for plotting
pca_df['cluster_info'] = pca_df['cluster'].map(cluster_labels)

# 4. Plot
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 7))
sns.scatterplot(
    data=pca_df.sort_values('cluster_info'), 
    x='PC1', y='PC2', 
    hue='cluster_info', 
    style='condition', 
    palette='viridis', 
    s=120
)

plt.title(f'K-Means Clustering (k={k}) on PCA Space\n(Annotated with Cluster Composition)')
plt.legend(bbox_to_anchor=(1.05, 1), loc='upper left', title="Cluster Composition")
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

# t-test Code

```{r gene-by-gene inference via t-test code (figure 7 in paper)}
# 1. Create a results storage table
gene_names <- rownames(clean_data)
inference_results <- data.frame(
  gene = gene_names,
  p_value = NA,
  log2FoldChange = NA,
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

# 2. Loop through each gene and run a t-test
# This is "Gene-by-Gene" inference in action!
for (i in 1:nrow(clean_data)) {
  # Separate the expression values for this specific gene
  healthy_values <- clean_data[i, 1:43]
  t1d_values <- clean_data[i, 44:82]
  
  # Calculate Log2 Fold Change (Difference in means)
  inference_results$log2FoldChange[i] <- mean(t1d_values) - mean(healthy_values)
  
  # Run the t-test
  t_test <- t.test(t1d_values, healthy_values)
  inference_results$p_value[i] <- t_test$p.value
}

# 3. Multiple Testing Correction (CRITICAL STEP)
# This converts raw p-values to Adjusted P-values (FDR)
inference_results$padj <- p.adjust(inference_results$p_value, method = "BH")

# 4. View the top significant genes
top_hits <- inference_results[order(inference_results$padj), ]
head(top_hits)

library(ggplot2)

# 1. Create a column to categorize genes for coloring
inference_results$diffexpressed <- "Not Significant"
# Thresholds: padj < 0.05 and absolute log2FoldChange > 0.5
inference_results$diffexpressed[inference_results$padj < 0.05 & inference_results$log2FoldChange > 0.5] <- "Up-regulated"
inference_results$diffexpressed[inference_results$padj < 0.05 & inference_results$log2FoldChange < -0.5] <- "Down-regulated"

# 2. Create the plot
ggplot(data=inference_results, aes(x=log2FoldChange, y=-log10(p_value), col=diffexpressed)) +
    geom_point(alpha=0.4, size=1.5) + 
    theme_minimal() +
    # Add vertical and horizontal lines for thresholds
    geom_vline(xintercept=c(-0.5, 0.5), col="black", linetype="dashed") +
    geom_hline(yintercept=-log10(0.05), col="black", linetype="dashed") +
    # Color coding
    scale_color_manual(values=c("Down-regulated" = "blue", "Not Significant" = "grey", "Up-regulated" = "red")) +
    labs(title="Volcano Plot: T1D vs Healthy",
         x="log2 Fold Change",
         y="-log10(p-value)")
```



```{r counting volcano plot genes}
# 1. Calculate the sum of just the significant genes (Up + Down)
# We exclude "Not Significant" from this sum
sig_total <- sum(summary_df$Gene_Count[summary_df$Expression_Status != "Not Significant"])

# 2. Create the "Total Significant" subtotal row
sig_row <- data.frame(
  Expression_Status = "Total Significant (DEGs)",
  Gene_Count = sig_total,
  Percentage = round((sig_total / sum(summary_df$Gene_Count)) * 100, 2)
)

# 3. Assemble the table in a logical "Storytelling" order:
# Individual DEGs -> Subtotal -> Not Significant -> Grand Total
final_summary_table <- rbind(
  summary_df[summary_df$Expression_Status == "Down-regulated", ],
  summary_df[summary_df$Expression_Status == "Up-regulated", ],
  sig_row,
  summary_df[summary_df$Expression_Status == "Not Significant", ],
  total_row
)

# 4. View your nice, organized table
print(final_summary_table)
```

# Random Forest Code

```{python code for random forest with LOOCV using scikit-learn (figure 9 in paper)}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import LeaveOneOut, cross_val_score
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, cohen_kappa_score

# load filtered data (18,015 genes)
data = pd.read_csv('/Users/gloriajackson/Desktop/26_spring/binf_6310-stats/vst_normalized_counts.csv', index_col=0)
metadata = pd.read_csv('/Users/gloriajackson/Desktop/26_spring/binf_6310-stats/sample_metadata.csv', index_col=0)

# transpose so samples are rows (82) and genes are columns (18,015)
X = data.T
y = metadata['condition']

# 3. Setup Random Forest
rf = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=500, max_features='sqrt', random_state=42)

# 4. Run Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOOCV)
loo = LeaveOneOut()
y_true = []
y_pred = []

print("Starting LOOCV... this may take a minute with 18k genes.")

for train_index, test_index in loo.split(X):
    X_train, X_test = X.iloc[train_index], X.iloc[test_index]
    y_train, y_test = y.iloc[train_index], y.iloc[test_index]
    
    rf.fit(X_train, y_train)
    y_pred.append(rf.predict(X_test)[0])
    y_true.append(y_test.iloc[0])

# 5. Calculate Final Metrics
accuracy = accuracy_score(y_true, y_pred)
kappa = cohen_kappa_score(y_true, y_pred)

print(f"--- Results for ALL 18,015 Filtered Genes ---")
print(f"Accuracy: {accuracy:.4f}")
print(f"Kappa: {kappa:.4f}")
```


#### Disclaimer: AI was used to help generate some code.
